lR;eso ;rs FOREWORD Dr. .. Jat Secretary (DARE) & Director General (ICAR) It is with great pleasure that present the “ICAR Kharif Agro-Advisories for Farmers 2025”- timely and comprehensive guide developed to empower India' farming community with science-based, region-speci�ic advisories for the upcoming Kharif season. This publication marks signi�icant step toward strengthening climate- resilient agriculture and enhancing farm productivity across diverse agro-climatic zones. Agriculture in India stands at critical juncture. While it continues to serve as the backbone of rural livelihoods, it faces unprecedented challenges due to climate variability, resource degradation, pest dynamics, and market volatility. In this context, the role of timely and location-speci�ic agro-advisories has become more crucial than ever. These advisories act as bridge between cutting-edge agricultural research and its practical application in farmers' �ields. This publication offers practical guidance on region-based suitable varieties, nutrient management, pest and disease control, and harvesting practices tailored for major Kharif crops such as rice, maize, pulses, oilseeds, and cotton. It is the result of collaborative efforts by ICAR Institutes, Agricultural Universities, Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs), and Subject Matter Experts working at the grassroots level. At ICAR, we remain committed to the vision of knowledge-driven, inclusive, and sustainable agricultural system. The advisories compiled herein re�lect our continuous efforts to translate scienti�ic knowledge from laboratories into actionable insights for every farmer. urge extension of�icers, KVK teams, farmer producer organizations (FPOs), and progressive farmers to widely disseminate and implement the recommendations provided in this book. extend my heartfelt appreciation to all scientists, experts, and �ield professionals who have contributed to the preparation of this publication. am con�ident that this agro-advisory compilation will serve as valuable resource for farmers during upcoming Kharif season, enabling them to make informed decisions that lead to improved productivity, pro�itability, and sustainability. Let us move forward together to build resilient, prosperous, and empowered farming community #Viksit Bharat 2047. Jai Kisan, Jai Vigyan! (.. Jat) ()

PREFACE Dr. Rajbir Singh Deputy Director General (Agricultural Extension), ICAR India' agricultural landscape is rich in diversity, yet increasingly in�luenced by climatic uncertainties, resource constraints, and changing socio-economic dynamics. To address these challenges effectively, there is growing need to equip farmers with timely, localized, and scienti�ically validated information that enhances their decision-making capacity. This compilation “ICAR Kharif Agro-Advisories for Farmers 2025” is well-conceived effort in this direction. This book compiles region-speci�ic advisories that re�lect the latest research and �ield-level insights from ICAR institutes, Agricultural Universities, and Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs) across the country. It is designed to serve as practical guide for extension personnel, progressive farmers, farmer producer organizations (FPOs), and agri-entrepreneurs engaged in Kharif crop planning and management. The advisories encompass timely, location-speci�ic, and scienti�ically backed recommendations and cover major crops, livestock, and �isheries, with focus on improving productivity, pro�itability, and sustainability. At ICAR, we believe that technology transfer and knowledge dissemination are as vital as research itself. The role of the extension system, particularly KVKs, has been pivotal in bridging the gap between research institutions and farming communities. This book is result of collaborative efforts between research and extension systems and exempli�ies our commitment to farmer-centric innovation. congratulate the scientists, editors, and extension professionals involved in compiling this publication. also encourage �ield functionaries and stakeholders to use this resource extensively to support farmers during the Kharif season. Let us continue to work together to transform Indian agriculture into more resilient, productive, and sustainable sector. (Rajbir Singh) (vii)

INTRODUCTION Agriculture remains the cornerstone of India' economy, supporting nearly half of the population and providing employment, nutrition, and rural development. The Kharif season, spanning from June to October, is particularly vital due to its reliance on the south-west monsoon. Approximately 55% of India' net sown area is rainfed, making the timely and equitable distribution of monsoon rains crucial for agricultural success. In 2025, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) forecasts an above-normal monsoon season, with cumulative rainfall expected at 105% of the Long Period Average (LPA) of 87 cm. The monsoon' onset is anticipated to be slightly delayed in southern India but should reach Maharashtra by May 27, facilitating timely sowing preparations. The IMD' forecast of an above-normal monsoon offers promising outlook but still carries risks of intra-seasonal dry spells and localized �looding. The Indian government' initiatives, such as the "Panchayat Mausam Seva," aim to deliver hyper-local weather forecasts at the panchayat level, ensuring that farmers receive accurate and timely information. The Kharif season witnesses the cultivation of diverse array of crops across India' agro-climatic zones, including rice, maize, cotton, soybean, groundnut, bajra, jowar, tur, urad, and moong. Despite advancements in agricultural technology, the sector remains vulnerable to climatic �luctuations such as delayed rains, uneven distribution, and extreme weather events. These challenges can adversely affect crop yields, farm incomes, and rural livelihoods. To mitigate these risks, it is imperative to provide farmers with location-speci�ic, research-backed guidance. Agro-advisories play pivotal role by offering timely, region-speci�ic, science-based information on weather patterns, crop management, pest control, harvesting, and storage. These advisories integrate seasonal outlooks with crop calendars and state-wise sowing windows, enabling farmers to select appropriate crop varieties and sowing dates, optimize resource use, and prevent yield losses. This advisory serves as crucial tool for agricultural of�icers, Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), and NGOs involved in rural development. By bridging the information gap between research and practice, it empowers farmers with the knowledge needed to make informed decisions, thereby contributing to the vision of self-reliant, prosperous, and inclusive India @ VIKSIT BHARAT 2047. (ix)

INTRODUCTION Agriculture remains the cornerstone of India' economy, supporting nearly half of the population and providing employment, nutrition, and rural development. The Kharif season, spanning from June to October, is particularly vital due to its reliance on the south-west monsoon. Approximately 55% of India' net sown area is rainfed, making the timely and equitable distribution of monsoon rains crucial for agricultural success. In 2025, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) forecasts an above-normal monsoon season, with cumulative rainfall expected at 105% of the Long Period Average (LPA) of 87 cm. The monsoon' onset is anticipated to be slightly delayed in southern India but should reach Maharashtra by May 27, facilitating timely sowing preparations. The IMD' forecast of an above-normal monsoon offers promising outlook but still carries risks of intra-seasonal dry spells and localized �looding. The Indian government' initiatives, such as the "Panchayat Mausam Seva," aim to deliver hyper-local weather forecasts at the panchayat level, ensuring that farmers receive accurate and timely information. The Kharif season witnesses the cultivation of diverse array of crops across India' agro-climatic zones, including rice, maize, cotton, soybean, groundnut, bajra, jowar, tur, urad, and moong. Despite advancements in agricultural technology, the sector remains vulnerable to climatic �luctuations such as delayed rains, uneven distribution, and extreme weather events. These challenges can adversely affect crop yields, farm incomes, and rural livelihoods. To mitigate these risks, it is imperative to provide farmers with location-speci�ic, research-backed guidance. Agro-advisories play pivotal role by offering timely, region-speci�ic, science-based information on weather patterns, crop management, pest control, harvesting, and storage. These advisories integrate seasonal outlooks with crop calendars and state-wise sowing windows, enabling farmers to select appropriate crop varieties and sowing dates, optimize resource use, and prevent yield losses. This advisory serves as crucial tool for agricultural of�icers, Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), and NGOs involved in rural development. By bridging the information gap between research and practice, it empowers farmers with the knowledge needed to make informed decisions, thereby contributing to the vision of self-reliant, prosperous, and inclusive India @ VIKSIT BHARAT 2047. (ix)

Andaman & Nicobar Islands The Andaman and Nicobar Islands have warm, humid tropical climate with high annual rainfall and fertile loamy soils, making them well-suited for diverse agricultural activities. The climatic conditions are ideal for cultivating paddy along with plantation crops such as coconut and arecanut. Major fruit crops include banana, mango, sapota, papaya, pineapple, and citrus, while vegetables like brinjal, okra, chilli, cowpea and cucurbits, tuber crops- tapioca, sweet potato, dioscorea and elephant foot yam as well as spices-black pepper, cinnamon, turmeric and ginger are also widely grown. CEREAL CROPS Rice Recommended varieties of medium duration are Dweep Dhan ,,, and , Jaya, ANR-47, and Gayatri whereas long duration varieties are Dweep Dhan , and ANR 40. Soak paddy seeds in normal water for 12-24 hours, discard the �loating seeds, and use only the fully submerged seeds for sowing. It is recommended to treat seeds with Bijamrut @ 300 ml/kg seed. About 1000 m² nursery area is adequate for one hectare of �ield. Apply FYM @ 10q or vermicompost @ along with along Azospirillum @ kg/1000 m² at - days prior to sowing in nursery. For nursery, prepare beds of 10 × .25 ; apply 20-30 kg FYM per bed. Maintain 30 cm drainage channel between beds. Sow 650-1000 of sprouted seed per bed. Maintain saturated condition of - cm standing water - days before uprooting seedlings. Before transplanting, root dipping of seedlings with Jeevamrit @ 50 ml/ may be followed for better establishment. In main �ield, spacing should be maintained by 20 cm 20 cm in ICM and 25 cm 25 cm for SRI methods. Apply either FYM@ . /ha or vermicompost @. /ha in the main �ield. Cono-weeder can be used for weeding purpose. Ensure bunds are repaired and 20-30 cm high to retain water. Use neem-based formulations (@ .% or ml/) - times for pest control.

Exposure to wet and cold conditions can lead to stress, reduced feed intake, and increased risk of diseases like foot rot and respiratory infections. Regularly monitor for signs of illness to ensure timely treatment. If any sign of illness is suspected, the pig should be isolated and treated. Provide dry and Tomato cultivation by using black Cultivation of king chilli under low cost suf�icient litter to keep them warm in the brooding area. Provide adequate clean polythene as mulching material polyhouse conditions drinking water with electrolyte and ad libitum feeding. Ensure proper vaccination In case of brinjal, farmers can grow long variety such as Pusa Purple Cluster, Borbengena, of birds against Ranikhet disease within the age of -7days old. Repeat the same JC-, Kuchia and BWR-34; round variety Pusa Purple Round, Pusa Vairab, BWR-12 with when chicks become 28 days old. Deworm the birds with fenbendazole @ 30- seed rate of 700-800 /ha. The ideal spacing for brinjal cultivation is 75 cm × 60 cm (row 40ml/100 birds in drinking water. Repeat the same after 21 days. Multivitamins to row and plant to plant). may be added in drinking water every once-twice in week. Provide warm, dry and mold free feed. Wet and humid conditions may increase the risk of coccidiosis and Farmers are advised to use low-cost rain shelters for raising the vegetable seedlings. respiratory disorders. If any disease outbreak occurs, maintain 45days of Pseudomonas. Biodegradable plastic mulching should be used in king chilly and brinjal crops for soil conservation and weed management. The Ginger/Turmeric �ield should be well prepared having drainage facility to prevent the water stagnation causing rhizome rot. Farmers are advised to spray neem-based formulation @ - ml/ of water or Bacillus thuringinsis @ 2g/ or Metarhiziumanisopliae @ 5ml/ for insects and pests and copper oxychloride @ .25% diseases management. To control the white �ly in king chilly neem oil (1500 ppm) spray during initial stage of infestation is recommended. Wash and disinfect the pens at regular basis and avoid dampness. Administer iron dextran in newly born piglets to prevent anemia. Assam Assam Transplanting: One ploughing should be given at least 21 days prior to transplanting. An irrigation for land soaking should be applied before preparatory tillage. The �inal puddling should be done - days prior to transplanting. One irrigation should be applied before �inal puddling. 25 days old seedlings of short and medium duration varieties should be transplanted by maintaining row-to-row and plant-to-plant spacing of 20 cm × 15 cm. In case of long duration varieties, 35-40 days old seedlings are to be transplanted at 25 cm × 15 cm spacing. It is advised to plant - seedlings per hill, at depth of - cm, for all varieties. Nutrient management: Well rotten FYM or compost @ 10 /ha has to be applied during �ield preparation. In addition, for semidwarf varieties, 60 kg /ha, 20 kg P₂O₅/ha and 40 kg K₂O/ha and for tall varieties, 20 kg /ha, 10 kg P₂O₅/ha and 10 kg K₂O/ are to be applied in areas with moderate fertility level. In case of poor soil, the rates of fertilizers may be required to increase to the extent of 60:30:30 kg/ha , P₂O₅ and K₂O respectively. Half of urea and whole of super phosphate and muriate of potash should be applied at the time of �inal puddling. Of the remaining part of urea, half at tillering stage .. 20-30 days after transplanting and other half at panicle initiation stage should be applied. Two weedings should be given with paddy weeder or hoe at 20 and 40 days after CEREAL CROPS transplanting. For weed control, pretilachlor @ .75 kg/ha or anilofos is to be applied @ . kg/ha at days after transplanting. In sali rice, application of cm irrigation water Sali rice days after disappearance of ponding water is recommended in medium and heavy soils. ICAR Kharif Agro-Advisory 2025 Chhatisgarh vaccination schedule should be followed before the onset of rainy season for prevention of Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD), Haemorrhagic Septicaemia (HS), Black Quarter (BQ) in cattle and buffaloes, and Peste des Petits Ruminants (PPR) in goats. Poultry must be protected from Ranikhet and fowl pox. To increase the feed intake in high yielding dairy animal during heat stress, farmers should practice urea treatment of dry straw ( kg urea in 40 litres water per 100 kg straw) to enhance its digestibility. Supplement animal diets with mineral mixture and common salt, and in lactating animals, give bypass protein and calcium supplements to maintain milk production. Ensure availability of green fodder during this season. Cultivate fodder crops like Maize, Sorghum, Cowpea and Napier to provide fresh and nutritious feed. In goats and sheep, provide bamboo slat �looring or dry raised platforms to prevent foot rot during wet months. Clean water should be provided to the animals three times day. Farmers are advised to administer Electrol Powder 2g and Vimeral ml per litre water for adults. In case of disease outbreak in animals, wash the infected animal tongue, mouth, feet and nose by % red potash or % baking soda - times in day. For adult birds, mix of Electrol powder and ml of Vimeral in litres of water. If the chick shows symptoms like drowsiness, cough and distorted feet appearing or change in colour CEREAL CROPS of excreta then immediately consult the nearest Veterinarian. Rice Fisheries Field preparation: Cleaning of the �ield and bund management should be done Fisherman are advised to prepare their own feed from locally available materials like before the monsoon, and summer ploughing to done which helps to destroy oil cake, undecomposed grains, rice bran, mineral salt etc. (To prepare 100 kg �ish pathogens and weed seeds. feed there is need of 40 kg oil cake, 19 kg undecomposed grain (�loor), 40 kg rice bran Seed rate: Paddy seeds should be treated with 17% salt solution before raising the and kg mineral mixture). In order to maintain the water colour light green, �ill the nursery or direct seeding. Use the recommended seed rate: for line sowing, 80-90 pond with fresh water as and when needed. Also to maintain the proper water kg/ha; for the Biasi and broadcast methods, 100-120 kg/ha. For the transplanting quality, apply lime @250 kg/ha and Potassium permagnate 500g/ha. Harvest only method, prepare the nursery in approximately /10�� of the total �ield area. Use 50 that much quantity which can be sold locally. Beware of gathering of �ish while kg/ha of seed for thick-grained varieties and 40 kg/ha for thin-grained varieties. catching and selling the �ish and at any cost maintain the social distancing. Varieties: Farmers are advised to grow high-yielding varieties rice varieties for sowing in the Kharif season, such as Vikram-TCR, Durgeshwari, Chhattisgarh Sugandhit Bhog, Indira Aerobic-, CG-1919, MTU-1318, CG Devbhog. Paddy Seeds should be treated with Azospirillum and PSB @ -10 /kg of seed before sowing. Line sowing method should be adopted in paddy cultivation and pre-emergence herbicide to be sprayed three days after sowing. Line sowing can be done using seed-cum-fertilizer drill in well-pulverized �ield. In leveled �ields, sowing should be carried out at row spacing of 20 cm using implements such as tractor-drawn seed drill, Indira seed drill, Nari plough, or Bhoramdev seed drill. The seeding depth should not exceed - cm. Proper calibration of the seed drill is essential to maintain the recommended seed rate accurately. Soil testing: Farmers are advised to get their soil tested. For this, they should contact Diagnostic visit to pond the local agricultural of�icer or nearby Krishi Vigyan Kendra. Based on the soil test results, balanced fertilizers should be applied accordingly. Weed management: Paddy should be sown in line and pre-emergence herbicide like Pretilachlor 50% EC @1500 ml/ha or Pyrazosulfuron 10% WP@ 200 ml/ha 38 39

Gujarat provides region-speci�ic, science-based recommendations to help farmers improve productivity and resilience during the monsoon season. With climate variability and resource constraints in mind, it promotes integrated crop management, timely pest-disease control, ef�icient water-nutrient use, and adoption of improved seed varieties. Tailored to the state' varied soils and rainfall patterns, this guidance aims to support sustainable farming and secure farmer livelihoods across Gujarat. SOUTH SAURASHTRA This region includes Amreli, Bhavnagar, Botad, Devbhumi-Dwarka, Gir-Somnath, Jamnagar, Junagadh, Morbi, Porbandar, Rajkot, and Surendranagar, SAU: Junagarh Agricultural University, Junagarh (Gujarat). They have an average rainfall of 400- 1000 mm. Soils are shallow to medium black soils, often affected by salinity and alkalinity. Groundnut Groundnut cultivation begins with deep ploughing the �ield - times to achieve �ine tilth up to 25-30 cm depth. Incorporating - /ha of well-decomposed farmyard manure (FYM) before sowing improves soil fertility. Sowing is generally done during June-July. Recommended semi-spreading varieties include GJG-22, GG-20, and Girnar-, while erect types include GJG-32, GJG-17, and KDG-128. For optimal plant spacing, maintain 60 × 10 cm for semi-spreading types and 45 × 10 cm for erect ones. Seeds should be treated with Vitavax and Rhizobium at 375-500 /ha to promote healthy germination and nitrogen �ixation. Weed control involves pre-emergence application of Pendimethalin at . kg/ha, followed by two hand weedings at 20 and 40 days after sowing (DAS), or alternatively, post-emergence application of Quizalofop--ethyl at 50 /ha. Timely irrigation is crucial, especially during �lowering, pegging, and pod development stages in case of dry spells. 48 49

Himachal Pradesh Himachal Pradesh DEMONSTRATION OF METHOD OF SOIL SAMPLING IN FIELD Use of Compost/Farm Yard Manure (FYM): Apply compost or Farm Yard Manure (FYM) at least weeks before sowing to improve soil fertility and water retention. Enriching FYM with bio-agents like Trichoderma and Pseudomonas enhances its effectiveness. Post-Harvest Storage: Ensure grains, pulses and millets are thoroughly dried before storage. Avoid reusing old gunny bags; instead, use treated bags soaked in per cent neem solution or insecticide to prevent pest infestation. Pro Tray Seedling Production: Adopt pro tray seedling technology using coco peat, perlite and vermiculite mixture for crops like tomato, brinjal, capsicum, and cauli�lower. This method ensures healthy, uniform seedlings, reduces disease risk, supports timely transplanting, and is especially suitable for women farmers. Snails attack: To prevent snail attacks, maintain clean and weed-free bunds. In case of infestation, apply metaldehyde baits as control measure. Rainwater Harvesting: Farmers are advised to adopt low-cost rainwater harvesting through silpaulin-lined ponds. These structures are easy to construct, cost-effective, and help store rainwater for irrigation during dry spells. Select low-lying area, dig pond, Himachal Pradesh is climatically diverse hill state where various crops are and line it with durable silpaulin sheet to minimize seepage. This method enhances cultivated according to the distinct climatic and geographical conditions. To ensure water availability during lean period and supports sustainable farming practices. the successful cultivation of these crops, it is essential to educate farmers about scienti�ic farming technique through agro-advisories. Marigold production under open �ields LIVESTOCK Ensuring structural soundness of livestock shelters is the �irst line of defense against environmental stress and disease. Shelters must have adequate slope for drainage, non-slippery �loors, and proper cross-ventilation. In �lood-prone zones, raised platforms or elevated sheds are essential to minimize moisture-related diseases such as foot rot, mastitis, and parasitic infestations. In cold and snow-bound areas- especially in the upper reaches-thermal insulation using mud plaster, dung, or dual- layer roo�ing provides essential protection. Weekly sanitation of poultry sheds with % sodium hypochlorite and fumigation using formalin with potassium permanganate ensures hygienic housing and microbial control. ICAR Kharif Agro-Advisory 2025 Jammu & Kashmir SPECIAL ADVISORY FOR COLD ARID ZONES (LAHAUL-SPITI & KINNAUR) In high-altitude cold arid regions, livestock housing must be thermally insulated using dung plaster, mud walls, and locally available materials to conserve body heat during snow-laden months. Animals should be fed dry grasses, willow leaves, and barley, with supplementation of mineral bricks and salt to prevent de�iciencies. Post-snowmelt, monitoring of yaks and donkeys for respiratory illness, especially pneumonia, is critical. Preventive vaccinations should be completed prior to onset of winter and again before transhumant herds migrate to alpine grazing pastures. JAMMU Mineral mixture & Hands-on training on drum silage Jammu region has three agro-climatic zones; sub-tropical (upto 800 above msl), Urea Molasses Mineral Brick preparation by CSKHPKV-KVK Mandi sub-temperate (800-1500m above msl) and temperate (above 1500m above msl). Rice is the major crop in irrigated plains, while maize is generally grown in hills under rainfed ecosystem. After Rabi harvest, deep plough �ields to utilize high temperatures for killing soil-borne pathogen spores, and take preventive steps to avoid post-harvest pest infestation. The crop-wise agro-advisory for kharif season is presented as below: CEREAL CROPS Paddy Soak paddy seed in carbendazim 10 + streptocyclin / kg seed (in water) for 24 hrs before nursery sowing. In sub-tropical zone, use blast-resistant coarse/semi- Mineral mixture supplementation Demonstration on Azolla cultivation to �ine varieties (SJR-, Jaya, PR-113, PHB-71, Ratna) and super�ine types (Jammu women farmers by CSKHPKV-KVK Mandi Basmati-129, Pusa Basmati 1121). For temperate/sub-temperate areas, prefer early, blast and cold-tolerant types like China-1039, Giza-14, -84, -343, -39. LIVESTOCK Ensure optimal health and hygiene in livestock and poultry by adopting best practices in animal husbandry, including proper feeding, clean and dry housing, adequate ventilation, and access to clean water. Monitor livestock and poultry regularly, at least twice daily, for any signs of abnormal behaviour, posture, feed intake, rumination, or irregular urination and defecation. Implement strict biosecurity measures by limiting visitor access and preventing the introduction and spread of diseases on the farm. Dispose of carcasses responsibly using methods such as burning, deep burial, or designated disposal pits, in compliance with health regulations. Deworm animals and poultry routinely for both internal and external parasites, in consultation with veterinarian, the State Animal Husbandry Department, or the Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK). Follow comprehensive vaccination program for poultry (Ranikhet disease, IBD, Fowl Pox, and Marek' disease), pigs (Swine Fever), and cattle/buffaloes (FMD, Black Quarter, and Haemorrhagic Septicaemia). FISHERIES To ensure effective and healthy �ish farming practices, proper pond preparation and management are crucial. Begin by applying lime (quick lime) at 200-300 kg/ha/year 166 167

SOIL TESTING Soil testing is the best tool to ensure optimum and balanced use of nutrients. Improper use of fertilizers leads not only to imbalanced nutrition but also deterioration of the environment. For collection of soil sample scrap away surface litter and make -shaped cut with spade or khurpa to depth of inches. Remove about " thick uniform slice of soil from one side of the cut. Take approximately half kg of soil in cloth bag and label it with information such as �ield number, name of the farmer, address, date of sampling etc. If the samples are wet, dry them in shade before putting into the cloth bag. ICAR Kharif Agro-Advisory 2025 Tamil Nadu & Puducherry In case of Goats, ensure deworming of goats (Albendazole @ . mg/kg body weight) especially young kids before the start of rainy season. Pig breed like HDK-75 is recommended for Sikkim. Pig farmers are advised for regular supplementation of mineral mixture @50-60 gm/day for pregnant sow and calcium to the lactating sow @ 60-80 ml/day. Dairy cattle farmers are advised to feed edible tree fodders as well as chopped paddy straw regularly to sustain the production. Also suggested for regular supplementation of mineral mixture @50-60 /day for adult cows and calcium to the lactating and pregnant cows @60-80 ml/day is very much essential for milking cows for getting optimum production and productivity. Restrict the movement of visitors into the farms and follow strict bio-security measures. Use of good disinfectant in foot bath. Vetiver Grass for Tannery-Polluted Soils: Used with organic composts to reduce chromium (21.%) and enhance carbon (25%) with by-product value. Sesuvium portulacastrum: Salt-tolerant species for reclaiming saline soils and improving physical and chemical soil properties. TNAU Panchagavya: % foliar spray of organic growth promoter containing nutrients and hormones; boosts yield by 25-30%, improves quality, and acts as pest repellent. For Horticultural Crops: Fruit Fly Management: IIHR' Arka Dorsolure- and kairomone traps targeting Bactrocera dorsalis (gravid females). Seed Treatment: Use TNAU seed coating formulation, dry, and sow accordingly. Drought Mitigation: Apply Nano Revive @ 10 ml/ at drought onset. Zinc Solubilizing Bacteria ( /ha) + Zinc Sulphate (25 kg/ha) + RDF; Bacillus altitudinis FD48 improves drought resilience. Mung bean and urd bean should be sown with the onset of monsoon, typically from June to July, as both early and late sowings can negatively affect crop performance. Early sowing may lead to maturity during the rainy period, causing losses, while late sowing results in poor crop growth and higher disease incidence. During the rainy season, both crops are often broadcast using seed rate of 15 kg/ha, but line sowing is recommended as it provides better canopy management and facilitates intercultural operations. Maintain row spacing of 30-45 cm depending on the sowing time and variety, with 15 kg/ha seed rate being optimal for effective plant stand and spacing. Before sowing, treat seeds with Agrosan GN or Thiram @ . /kg of seed to prevent seed-borne diseases. Groundnut (TG 37A) at Mainpuri Sesame (GJT ) at Amethi Inoculate seeds with appropriate Rhizobium culture, especially if urd bean is being second three weeks later, as this helps minimize early weed competition, promotes better aeration, and supports healthy crop growth. For additional control, especially under high weed pressure, apply pre-emergence herbicide imazethapyr @ 100 /ha in 500-600 liters of water within days of sowing. Soybean Cultivate improved soybean varieties such as PK-472, JS-715, PS-564, PK-262, JS-, JS- 935, JS-7244, JS 20-29, Pusa-20, Pusa-16, and PK-416 based on growing season and production goals. The crop matures in about 120-125 days; sow between mid-June and the �irst fortnight of July. Maintain row spacing of 45 cm and plant-to-plant spacing of - cm; sow seeds - cm deep. Use 70-75 kg seed/ha under normal conditions and 75- 80 kg/ha under late sowing. Ensure minimum germination of 75-80%. Greengram (Virat) at Etawah Blackgram (Shekhar ) at Kaushambhi 258 259
